title: Four-shaft huck lace scarf
subtitle: A fine cotton scarf woven in a classic huck lace threading on four shafts, approximately 160 x 18 cm
excerpt: Huck lace is a four-shaft structure in which weft floats form at regular intervals across the cloth, producing an open, airy fabric with a lace-like surface texture. This scarf in fine mercerised cotton is a good first huck lace project: the threading is straightforward, and the structure clearly reveals how float groups create the lace effect.
sourceNotes: Huck lace threading and treadling are documented in Ashenhurst's 'Theory of Textiles' and in standard four-shaft weave structure references including Osterkamp's 'Weaving for Beginners'. The scarf specifications are synthesised from UK guild project sheets.

glossaryTerms.definition: A four-shaft weft-float lace structure in which a recurring threading unit of one tabby thread and one pattern thread on each shaft creates groups of floating weft at regular intervals. The floats open the cloth surface to produce a lace-like texture without cutting the weft.

A length of weft (or warp) that passes over two or more threads on the opposite face without interlacing. In huck lace, controlled groups of weft floats form the open lace pattern. Floats must be short enough to avoid snagging in use.

The opening between raised and lowered warp threads through which the shuttle passes to lay in the weft. Each treadle combination opens a different shed; huck lace uses a sequence of four or more distinct sheds to create the float groups.

content.text: is one of the most accessible four-shaft lace structures because the threading unit is short (four threads) and the sequence of
follows a predictable alternating pattern. Each treadling sequence creates a row of weft

in one block and plain weave in the other, and the blocks swap position with each subsequent sequence. Mercerised cotton at fine-to-sport weight (2/8 or similar) shows the lace structure clearly and produces a scarf with good drape.

items.name: 2/8 mercerised cotton or sport weight cotton, main colour
Four-shaft floor loom or table loom, 12 to 15 dent reed

content.text: Wind the warp at 18 ends per 10 cm for a finished width of 18 cm (approximately 330 cm warp length to allow for draw-in and loom waste). The huck threading unit is a 4-thread repeat: shaft 1, shaft 2, shaft 1, shaft 3. Continue this 4-thread unit across the full width. Shaft 1 is the tabby thread that appears on every repeat; shafts 2 and 3 carry the pattern threads. Shaft 4 is not used in a two-block huck draft; leave it empty or use it for selvedge threads if desired. Thread the reed at 15 ends per 10 cm, sleying some dents with 2 ends and others with 1 to distribute the ends evenly.

Tie the treadles as follows: treadle A lifts shafts 1 and 3; treadle B lifts shaft 2 alone; treadle C lifts shafts 1 and 2; treadle D lifts shaft 3 alone. The huck lace treadling sequence is A, B, A, B for block 1 (floats on the shafts 2 pattern threads), then C, D, C, D for block 2 (floats on the shafts 3 pattern threads). Two passes of each treadle combination produce a square block at this sett. Alternate between the two block sequences without tabby rows between; the huck structure self-interlaces at the block boundaries.

Remove the scarf from the loom and secure the warp ends with hemstitching woven in before cutting off, or tie in groups of four with overhand knots. Hand wash in cool water with a gentle detergent, supporting the full length of the wet cloth. The huck lace structure opens further after washing as the weft relaxes into the float gaps. Press under a damp cloth while the scarf is slightly damp, using the iron carefully to avoid flattening the lace texture.

items.problem: The lace pattern is not visible and the cloth looks like plain weave
items.cause: The treadling sequence is incorrect or the tie-up has the wrong shafts on each treadle
items.fix: Weave a short test section and look at the underside of the cloth. The weft floats should be clearly visible on the back. If the back also looks like plain weave, the tie-up does not match the huck sequence; check the shaft assignments against the threading draft.

items.problem: The float groups are very long and the cloth feels loose and unstable
items.cause: The sett is too open for the yarn weight
items.fix: Re-sley the reed at a tighter sett (add one additional end per dent across the width). Huck lace floats should span no more than 3 to 4 warp threads; longer floats indicate the warp is too spread for the yarn diameter.
